content:
# John Chapter 10 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 10 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 10:1 — "Truly, truly" vs. "I tell you the solemn truth" and "climbs in by another way" vs. "sneaks over the wall"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Amen, amen, I say to you, the [one] not entering through the door...but climbing up from another place, that [one] is a thief and a robber." | Literal; "Amen, amen" |
| LOGOS | "Truly, truly, I say to you, he who does not enter the sheepfold by the door but climbs in by another way, that man is a thief and a robber." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the solemn truth: anyone who sneaks over the wall of a sheepfold, rather than going through the gate, must surely be a thief and a robber!" | Dynamic; "sneaks"; "wall"; "gate"; "surely" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "sneaks" (implying stealth not in the Greek), "wall" (not specified), and "gate" for θύρα (which means "door"). The double-amen formula continues to be resolved in favor of "Truly, truly."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:6 — "figure of speech" vs. "illustration" for Greek παροιμίαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This figure of speech the Yeshua spoke to them" | "Figure of speech" for παροιμία |
| LOGOS | "This figure of speech Jesus used with them" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Those who heard Jesus use this illustration didn't understand what he meant" | "Illustration"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. παροιμία means "proverb/figure of speech/allegory" — "illustration" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but slightly less precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:7 — "I am the door" vs. "I am the gate"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am the door of the sheep" | "Door" for θύρα |
| LOGOS | "I am the door of the sheep" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I am the gate for the sheep" | "Gate" for θύρα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "door." θύρα means "door" (an opening in a barrier). "Gate" is acceptable but typically refers to an outdoor entrance. In a sheepfold context both work, but "door" is the traditional rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:10 — "have it abundantly" vs. "a rich and satisfying life" for Greek περισσὸν ἔχωσιν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I came that they may have life and have [it] abundantly" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "I came that they may have life and have it abundantly." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "My purpose is to give them a rich and satisfying life." | Dynamic; "rich and satisfying" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "rich and satisfying" domesticates περισσόν ("abundantly/exceedingly") into a lifestyle category. "Abundantly" preserves the open-ended excess.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:11 — "lays down his life" vs. "sacrifices his life" for Greek τίθησιν τὴν ψυχὴν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The good shepherd lays down the life of him for the sheep." | Literal; "lays down" |
| LOGOS | "The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep." | Formal; "lays down" |
| PNEUMA | "The good shepherd sacrifices his life for the sheep." | Dynamic; "sacrifices" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. τίθημι means "to place/lay down" — it has a deliberate, voluntary connotation. "Sacrifices" (PNEUMA) interprets the action but "lays down" preserves the unique Johannine idiom that is repeated four times in this chapter.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:12-13 — "hired hand" vs. "hired one" vs. detailed paraphrase

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The hired [one]...it does not matter to him concerning the sheep" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "He who is a hired hand and not a shepherd...cares nothing for the sheep" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "A hired hand will run when he sees a wolf coming...he's working only for the money and doesn't really care about the sheep." | Dynamic; "working only for the money" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "working only for the money" — an interpretive expansion. While the economic motivation is implied, the Greek says simply "because he is a hired hand" (ὅτι μισθωτός ἐστιν).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:18 — "This charge I have received" vs. "this is what my Father has commanded" for Greek ἐντολήν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "This commandment I received from the Father of me." | Literal; "commandment" |
| LOGOS | "This charge I have received from my Father." | Formal; "charge" |
| PNEUMA | "For this is what my Father has commanded." | Dynamic; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. ἐντολή means "commandment/charge/commission." "Charge" (LOGOS) is appropriate in this context — it suggests a commission or mandate rather than a legal command.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 10:20 — "He has a demon, and is insane" vs. "He's demon-possessed and out of his mind"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He has a demon and is mad." | Literal; "mad" for μαίνεται |
| LOGOS | "He has a demon, and is insane" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "He's demon-possessed and out of his mind. Why listen to a man like that?" | Dynamic; "demon-possessed"; adds "a man like that" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA again uses "demon-possessed" (modern clinical term). The Greek says "has a demon" (δαιμόνιον ἔχει). "Insane" (LOGOS) is closer to μαίνεται than "mad" (EMET) in modern English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:22 — "the Feast of Dedication" vs. "Hanukkah, the Festival of Dedication"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Then the Dedication happened in Hierosolyma. It was winter." | Literal; "Dedication" |
| LOGOS | "At that time the Feast of Dedication took place at Jerusalem. It was winter" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "It was now winter, and Jesus was in Jerusalem at the time of Hanukkah, the Festival of Dedication." | Dynamic; adds "Hanukkah"; reorders |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "Hanukkah" which, while historically accurate, is not in the Greek text. The Greek says τὰ ἐγκαίνια ("the Dedication").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:24 — "How long will you keep us in suspense?" rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Until when do you hold the soul of us [in suspense]?" | Literal; "hold the soul" |
| LOGOS | "How long will you keep us in suspense?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "How long are you going to keep us in suspense?" | Dynamic; slightly different tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. All three are close. EMET's "hold the soul" is the literal Greek idiom (τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν αἴρεις).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 10:25 — "bear witness about me" vs. "the proof is the work I do"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The works that I do in the name of the Father of me, these testify concerning me." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "The works that I do in my Father's name bear witness about me" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "The proof is the work I do in my Father's name." | Dynamic; "proof" instead of "bear witness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. μαρτυρέω means "bear witness/testify" — PNEUMA's "proof" is a modern legal term that changes the register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:30 — "I and the Father are one" rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I and the Father are one." | Literal; same word order as Greek |
| LOGOS | "I and the Father are one." | Formal; same |
| PNEUMA | "The Father and I are one." | Dynamic; reverses the order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS/EMET — "I and the Father are one." The Greek word order places ἐγώ first (ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατήρ), and this emphatic self-placement is characteristic of Jesus' speech in John. PNEUMA reverses the order.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET.

---

## 10:32 — "I have shown you many good works" vs. "At my Father's direction I have done many good works"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Many good works I showed you from the Father" | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "I have shown you many good works from the Father" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "At my Father's direction I have done many good works" | Dynamic; "direction" interprets "from the Father" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "at my Father's direction" interprets ἐκ τοῦ πατρός ("from the Father") as directive rather than origin.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:33 — "make yourself God" vs. "claim to be God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you, being a man, make yourself God" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "you, being a man, make yourself God" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "You, a mere man, claim to be God." | Dynamic; "mere man"; "claim to be" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "mere" (not in Greek) and changes "make yourself" (ποιεῖς σεαυτόν) to "claim to be" — a significant shift. The Greek accusation is about what Jesus is DOING (making himself = placing himself in the category of God), not merely what he is SAYING.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:38 — "believe the works" vs. "believe in the evidence of the miraculous works"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "believe the works" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "believe the works" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "believe in the evidence of the miraculous works I have done" | Dynamic; adds "evidence of" and "miraculous" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds both "evidence of" and "miraculous" — the Greek simply says "believe the works" (τοῖς ἔργοις πιστεύετε).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 10:39 — "he escaped from their hands" vs. "he got away and left them"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he went out of the hand of them" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "he escaped from their hands" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "he got away and left them" | Dynamic; casual |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Escaped" captures the tension. PNEUMA's "got away" is colloquial.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion | 9 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Double-amen formula | 1 | Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS |
| Word order reversal (PNEUMA) | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET |
| "Door" vs. "gate" | 1 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 10 contains the Good Shepherd discourse and the Dedication confrontation. The central theological phrase "I and the Father are one" (v.30) draws near-perfect consensus, with only PNEUMA reversing the word order. The most significant divergences are PNEUMA's rendering of "lays down his life" as "sacrifices his life" (losing the unique Johannine τίθημι idiom), "abundantly" as "a rich and satisfying life" (domesticating the excess), and "make yourself God" as "claim to be God" (shifting from action to speech). EMET's most distinctive contribution is preserving the emphatic Greek word order in v.30.

The chapter shows strong LOGOS-SOPHIA alignment on the shepherd imagery, with PNEUMA's dynamic rendering adding vivid but ultimately unnecessary words ("sneaks," "mere man," "miraculous") that are not in the Greek text.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
